pr.title: Fix issue with building `python_Levenshtein` on Windows
pr.author: ribbanya

summary: Updated Windows setup requirements so `python_Levenshtein` can build successfully: added `cmake` to documented and packaged Scoop installs, and relaxed the pinned `python_Levenshtein` requirement from exactly `0.20.9` to `>=0.20.9`.
what_changed: Added `cmake` to the Windows setup instructions in `.github/README.md` as a required Scoop package.
Updated the documented PATH prerequisite list to include `cmake` alongside `git`, `python`, `bash`, `gcc`, and `make`.
Added `cmake` to `.github/packages/build-windows/setup.ps1` so the Windows package setup installs it automatically.
smart_moves: Fixed both the automated Windows setup script and the human-facing README, avoiding divergence between CI/package setup and local developer instructions.
Addressed the likely build-tool dependency directly by adding `cmake` to Windows setup rather than relying on users to infer it from a Python package build failure.
Relaxed an overly strict Python dependency pin for `python_Levenshtein`, giving pip room to select a compatible newer release while preserving the existing minimum known version.
decomp_lessons: Tooling dependencies for decomp workflows should be captured in setup automation and documentation together, especially on Windows where Python packages may require native build tools.

When a Python requirement depends on native compilation, exact pins can increase platform-specific breakage; use a lower-bound constraint when newer compatible package releases may contain packaging or wheel fixes.
Small environment fixes can materially improve contributor onboarding even when they do not touch game code or matching work.
naming_conventions: The project uses the PyPI package name spelling `python_Levenshtein` in `requirements.txt`.
Windows setup documentation refers to Scoop package names in lowercase, e.g. `git`, `python`, `mingw`, `cmake`.
follow_up_queries: Check later PRs for additional Windows setup dependency updates after adding `cmake`.
Search for any remaining exact Python dependency pins that caused platform-specific install failures.
Verify whether `python_Levenshtein>=0.20.9` remains compatible with current tooling or should be replaced by a differently named package if upstream changed.
